زاعم أن طيف بدعة قد أندب
أجلبوا تحت غابة من قنا الخط ،
ليس من عصبة ، إذا استأنفوا السعي
بأبي أنت ، أنت أول من حولني
ما الندى في سواك غير حديث

translationEn: The poet opens with a meditation on youth and old age, wondering whether to wear the garment of youth or resign himself to white hair. He reflects bitterly on time, rivals, and the foolishness of Ibn Ayyub who recklessly brought troops against him. The ode pivots to praise of a patron whose generosity saved poetry itself from extinction, comparing his beneficence to spring rains that reveal hidden blossoms.
context: This poem blends personal elegy on aging with sharp satire of a rival before concluding with praise of a generous patron. Al-Buhturi's characteristic shift from intimate self-reflection to panegyric is on full display here.

titleEn: An Apology to Abu Ja'far

verses.sadr: وإذا ما أردت أن تمنع الناس
إنما كنت واردا في جميع الناس

translationEn: The poet addresses Abu Ja'far with an apology, noting that news of their friendship has spread through Manbij. He asks forgiveness for his long-standing offenses and describes Ibn al-Ghala'ili's company as gifting him beauty, song, and poetry alike. He concludes with the flattering comparison that trying to keep the people from the Euphrates would be as futile as denying his patron's generosity.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: traps, nets; or gathering places near water
one who grants access, who lets flow

context: A short poem of apology and affection addressed to Abu Ja'far, blending the erotic with the social. Al-Buhturi's light touch here reflects his mastery of the khafif meter for shorter, more personal compositions.

titleEn: My Only Condition Is Fairness
translationEn: The poet declares his only stipulation in friendship is fairness; he asks nothing beyond justice from other men. He addresses a nobleman of sound judgment, accusing someone of excess in their demands. He closes with a resigned philosophical observation: life's vicissitudes are like a star that rises then falls — he cannot be diminished by fate's arithmetic.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: grey hair mixed with black, old age

context: A brief reflective poem in the ramal meter on the poet's philosophy of fair dealing. Al-Buhturi presents himself as a man of principle who asks only for justice, not favor.

titleEn: Your Counsel O Abu Nahshal
translationEn: The poet humorously addresses Abu Nahshal, asking why he would desire the company of a crude man from Khuttal whose boorish behavior would harm fellow drinkers. The poem is a sharp lampoon against a rival poet Abu al-Barq, predicting his verses will be devoured and forgotten. The imagery is deliberately coarse and satirical.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you drink with him as a companion
the grey-haired man with a receding hairline

context: A satirical poem (hija') aimed at a rival poet, using the mutaqarib meter and deliberately crude imagery to demean the target. Al-Buhturi's satires, though less celebrated than his panegyrics, show considerable wit.

titleAr: ترى الليل يقضي عقبة من هزيعه
titleEn: Does the Night Still Have Hours Remaining

verses.sadr: ترى الليل يقضي عقبة من هزيعه ،
verses.ajuz: أم الصبح يجلو غرة من صديعه
على وجده ، أو زائد في ولوعه

verses.sadr: ولم تبن دار العجز للملحس الذي
وليس امرأ إلا امرؤ ذهبت به
وكان اختيال العلج من عطش الردى

verses.ajuz: وقد كان يكفي بعضه من جميعه

verses.sadr: ولم ينقلب من بست ، إلا ورأيه
فإن يحي لا يفلح ، وإن يثو لا يكن
دم إن يرق لا يقض تبلا مراقه ،
شفى برح الأكباد أن ابن طاهر

verses.ajuz: ولا يخف كافي شانها من مضيعه

verses.sadr: متى قظت في شرق البلاد ، فإنني
ومرضى من الحساد قد كان شفهم
وما عذرهم في أن تعل صدورهم
فلا عجب أن يطلب السيل نهجه ،

translationEn: The poem opens with lyrical reflections on the night, longing, and sleeplessness born of passionate love. The poet philosophizes on old age and the folly of rivals. The ode then praises Ibn Tahir and celebrates the hope that his leadership will bring light to Khorasan as a full moon rises from the west, routing enemies and silencing envious detractors.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a coppersmith; here a derogatory epithet

context: A long qasida that moves from amatory opening (nasib) through self-reflection and satire of a rival, then to praise of Ibn Tahir as a leader destined to illuminate Khorasan. The poem exemplifies the classical qasida structure.

titleEn: He Burdened Me Beyond My Capacity

verses.ajuz: في الرأي ، أن يأمر من لا يطيع

verses.sadr: طول هذا الليل أن لا كرى
verses.ajuz: يريك من تهوى وأن لا هجوع
فما كث عن حظه ، أو سريع

translationEn: The poet opens with complaints about a blamer who burdens him beyond capacity and commands him to forget his beloved Asma'. He philosophizes on patience, contentment, and the two kinds of wealth — giving and withholding. The ode then turns to lavish praise of Abu Ghanim, a patron of towering stature and terrifying power, whose generosity is as sweet as fresh musk and whose protection has been the poet's refuge throughout his life.
context: This lengthy qasida in the sari' meter follows the classical form: amatory prelude (nasib) lamenting Asma', followed by moral reflections, then an extended panegyric to Abu Ghanim. Al-Buhturi's description of the patron on horseback is among his finest descriptive passages.

titleEn: We Commend the Departing Traveler to God

verses.sadr: بنو مخلد إن يشرع الحمد يشرهوا
verses.ajuz: إليه ، وإن يدعوا إلى المجد يسرعوا

verses.sadr: إذا نحن شيعنا من القوم واحدا ،

translationEn: The poet reflects on the farewell of a departing traveler, whom they commend to God's protection. He praises Banu Mukhalid: when praise is initiated they rush toward it, and when called to glory they hasten. So strong is their bond of loyalty that when they escort one of their own away, they forsake sleep until he returns.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a departing traveler, one setting out

context: A brief three-verse fragment praising Banu Mukhalid for their fierce loyalty and eagerness to pursue glory. The poem is a concise jewel of panegyric in the tawil meter.

titleEn: The Brotherhood of Necessity Has Departed

verses.ajuz: بك القول شأوا رد منك فأسرعا

verses.sadr: وما أنا بالظمآن فيك إلى التي
أغار على ما بيننا أن يناله
verses.ajuz: لسان عدو لم يجد فيك مطمعا

verses.sadr: وكم حفرة في غور نجران أشفقت
ملكت عنان الهجر أن يبلغ المدى ،
فإن تدعني للشر أسرع ، وإن تهب

translationEn: The poet addresses an estranged companion whose friendship was born of necessity; that bond is now fraying. He says he started it but the wrong was plain, and his words drove the friend further away. Rather than letting enemies feast on their quarrel or unleashing angry verses, he held back the reins of estrangement. He ends with an ultimatum: if called to hostility he will rush there, but he has left room for reconciliation.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a friend of circumstance, not sincere brotherhood
I disdain, I find beneath dignity

context: A poem of complaint and guarded reconciliation addressed to an estranged friend. The poet balances wounded pride with a desire to preserve the relationship, revealing al-Buhturi's skill in navigating personal conflicts through verse.

titleEn: Al-Kumaiti the Elder Abandoned Us
translationEn: The elder Kumaiti abandoned the poet, and the younger Kumaiti offered no intercession. Neither brought comfort during their stay, even knowing that departure was imminent. The poet asks bitterly: if they severed the bond while living side by side, how could they ever reconnect when scattered apart?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: he abandoned us, turned away from us
an intercessor, one who pleads on another's behalf
separation is swift, departure comes quickly

context: A compact three-verse lament about two companions named al-Kumaiti — elder and younger — who abandoned the poet despite proximity. The poem's rhetorical power lies in its final paradox about distance and closeness.

titleEn: A Master Who Imposed Humiliation on Me

verses.sadr: إن كنت لا تنوي نجاحا لها ،
هل لك في الصلح ، فأعفيك من

translationEn: The poet addresses a patron who has imposed humiliation and broken a promise of a hundred dinars, repeatedly deferring payment. He invokes the faithfulness of Isma'il and Ibrahim as examples the patron has failed to match. He proposes a compromise: forgive half and pay the rest, or at least restore the friendship on equal footing — for a burden too heavy deserves to be exchanged for something lighter.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: imposed humiliation and injustice on me
taking his time, being slow about it

context: A frank poem of complaint about an unfulfilled monetary promise, blending indignation with humor and a pragmatic proposal for settlement. It reveals the real-world patronage pressures al-Buhturi navigated throughout his career.

titleEn: The Phantom of Mawiyya Visits Me
translationEn: The poet is kept awake by the phantom of Mawiyya, a beautiful woman with a weak waist weighed down by the dunes behind her. He shifts to praise of Abdun, a unique individual of the al-Harith ibn Ka'b tribe whose generosity is so great that notables of every people cluster at his gate. The poem ends with a charming moment of equality: the poet and the governor of al-Sawad were alike in that night, both guests of the same refined host.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the one who circles, the phantom that visits

context: A poem in the munsarih meter that opens with an erotic description of Mawiyya, pivots to praise of the generous Abdun, and ends with a memorable vignette of the poet dining beside the governor — both equally guests of this magnanimous host.

titleEn: Greetings to the Summer and Spring Abodes

verses.sadr: تهتز في هيف ، وما بعث الهوى
إذ لا ينهنهني العذول ولا أرى
صنع الصنائع في الرجال ، ولم يكن

translationEn: The poem opens with an address to the spring and summer dwellings of Zaynab and Saduf, the beloved women whose memory keeps the poet awake. He describes their slender figures with vivid imagery before resolving to end his lovesickness and turn to gratitude. He praises Abu Ali whose largesse established both his old and new wealth, and whose handsome son Wasif is like the full moon when he faces the poet's needs. He closes with a petition, confident that one good deed will be followed by another.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: shed tears, tears that pour down
a nobleman of fine stock, generous

context: A full classical qasida moving from nasib (amatory prelude at abandoned dwellings) through self-exhortation to abandon love, culminating in elaborate praise of Abu Ali and his luminous son Wasif. The poem closes with a refined petition for continued patronage.

titleEn: Hadramaut and Its Distant Tribes

verses.sadr: نحن من قد علمت في الشرف الوافي ،

translationEn: The poet addresses a rival with cutting irony, questioning whether he lampoons the poet's father or his own Khath'ami cobbler ancestor. He asserts his own tribe's noble lineage far surpasses any claim the rival might make and suggests that scrutinizing the rival's patriarch would tax even a money-changer's patience.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a cobbler, shoemaker (used as an insult)
a money-changer, one who scrutinizes coins

context: A brief poem of tribal boasting (fakhr) and counter-satire, asserting the poet's lineage against a rival's pretensions. The reference to Hadramaut's distance and the cobbler ancestor are classic devices of poetic invective.

titleEn: A Swaying Beauty Far in Affection

verses.sadr: إذا بعدت أبلت وإن قربت شفت ،
بذلت لها الود الذي بخلت به ،
verses.ajuz: وأصفيتها الود الذي لم تكن تصفي
وإن الذي أبدي لدون الذي أخفي

verses.sadr: أما يطمع المحروم عندك في الجدا ،
verses.ajuz: ولا يطمع المظلوم عندك في النصف
فأكرم به من ردء قوم ومن كهف
فلا ناقص الجدوى ولا جامد الكف
كما حن إلف مستهام إلى إلف

verses.sadr: ويقلق حتى ينجز الوعد مثل ما
verses.ajuz: يجافي الذي يمشي على رمض الرضف
من النيل ، أصبح في أمان من الخلف

verses.sadr: وما ألف ألف من جداك كثيرة ،
verses.ajuz: وكيف أخاف الفوت عندك في ألف

translationEn: The poem opens with the description of a swaying beauty — delicate of limb, languid of gaze — whose distance sickens and whose nearness heals. The poet confesses he gave her more love than she returned, and all he revealed was less than what he concealed. He pivots to praise of the Caliph, the shield of the Muslims, who longs to do good as ardently as a lovesick man longs for his companion. He ends with a confident plea: a thousand thousand of the Caliph's gifts is not too many, and surely a mere thousand is safe.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: swaying at the sides, gracefully moving
the refuge, cave of protection for Muslims

context: A qasida in the tawil meter structured classically: an erotic description of an unnamed beloved leads into praise of the Caliph as a man who yearns to be generous as intensely as a lover yearns for union. The closing petition for a thousand dinars is characteristic of al-Buhturi's frank patronage poetry.

titleEn: By the One Who Gave You Virtue and Power

verses.ajuz: على كل حي ، واصطفاك على الخلق
سنا الشمس من أفق ووجهك من أفق
سلكت بها نهج السبيل إلى الحق

translationEn: The poet swears by God who gave the Caliph virtue over all creation that his rule is one of justice, generosity, and gentleness. He sees the Prophet's mark in the Caliph's bright, open face and praises how he saved Homs and its people through clemency rather than the sword. His appearance before them was like two suns rising at once. The poem closes with the memorable image: the loyalty of those freed from destruction surpasses even the loyalty of those freed from slavery.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: they committed, they were guilty of

context: A panegyric to the Caliph al-Mutawakkil praising his pacification of Homs through clemency. The theological opening and comparison to the Prophet's radiance reflect al-Buhturi's ability to elevate political praise to a sacred register.

titleEn: I Said to the Blamer at the Well: Wake Up

verses.ajuz: لا تهون طعم شيء لم تذق

verses.sadr: لا يلذ الملتقى ، إن لم يكن

verses.ajuz: لم يقل إن المنايا في الحدق

verses.sadr: إن تكن محتسبا من قد ثوى
ليس فيه غير ما يغري به ،

verses.ajuz: من قبيح في رهان ، أو سبق

verses.sadr: علم في الإفك ، لو قال لنا

verses.ajuz: قد رعى في مسرح الذم وزق

verses.sadr: لو صفرنا عب في الماء ، ولو
إن مشى هملج ، أو صاح إلى
لا وظيف العير مرقوم ولا العجب

verses.ajuz: يتبرأ من عشى ، أو من سرق
أن أرى في أعين الحمر زرق
من لم يك في قعر النفق
فضل ما أوبق ميتا من غرق

verses.sadr: لو توفي ما لنا في صاعد ،

verses.ajuz: لا يرعك الحظ لم يؤخذ بحق
غصن إن لم يكن غض الورق

verses.sadr: لا يحب الخرق ، إلا في الوغى ،

translationEn: The poet opens by rebuking a blamer, philosophizing on sleeplessness, longing, and love. He then launches a ferocious satire against a contemptible rival — describing his baseness, blue eyes, unknown lineage, and worthless friendship in a cascade of insults. The qasida then pivots dramatically to praise of the Mukhladi family, elevating Sai'd as a generous, decisive, warrior-statesman of the Madhij tribe whose renown far outstrips his formal rank.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the well; also: the cistern of the heart
the guest of slumber, sleep's visitor
a spear from Khatt (famous for spear-making)
last breath of life, a thread of life

context: One of al-Buhturi's longest poems in this batch, combining satire and panegyric in the ramal meter. The savagely detailed portrait of the rival and the heroic portrait of the patron represent two poles of the classical Arabic qasida tradition at their most elaborate.

titleAr: أن رق لي قلبك مما ألاق
titleEn: If Your Heart Softened for My Suffering

verses.sadr: أن رق لي قلبك مما ألاق
من لم يبحك النصح من قلبه ،
verses.ajuz: فما له في دينه من خلاق

translationEn: The poet begs his beloved to soften her heart and grant a farewell kiss before departure, confessing he fears separation because of the pain but craves it for the embrace it requires. He pivots to praise of the Caliph Ja'far, whose obedience is a religious duty and whose survival ensures that good persists in the world. He then hymns Damascus as a paradise — its breezes fresh, its water sweet, its seasons fair — and urges the Caliph to prefer it over Iraq.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one deeply in debt, one enslaved by love
sweet water of the lightning-lit meadow

context: A poem that elegantly weaves amatory opening with political panegyric, praising the Caliph al-Mutawakkil (Ja'far) and advocating for Damascus. The description of Damascus as a paradise rivals al-Buhturi's celebrated descriptions of natural landscapes.

titleEn: Your Hands Drew Near with Noble Deeds

verses.sadr: ما كان صوب المزن يطمع قبلها
verses.ajuz: في أن يجيء نداه ، قبل نداكا
من طيب عرفك ، أو جميل ثناكا

translationEn: The poet praises Abu Nuh whose generous hands have drawn near to all who seek, comparing even spring to his brother because both bring abundance. He describes a gathering of young men waiting for the morning drink, whose wine seems to carry the scent of the patron's good repute. The cups pass and stories of his unique virtues replace the need for song entirely.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: your reach, your extent of giving

context: A refined symposium poem (khamriyya-tinged madih) for Abu Nuh, linking the pleasure of wine, the beauty of spring, and the patron's generosity into a single extended metaphor. The intimate drinking-session setting is characteristic of al-Buhturi's social poetry.

titleEn: Will You Hear the One Who Calls to You

verses.sadr: يا يوسف بن محمد دعوى امرىء ،
ما زلت مذ جاريت سابق معشر
صرفوك عن حرب الثغور بقدر ما
والروم تعلم أن سيفك لم يزل
أرض تتيه على السحاب إذا التقى
لم ترو دجلة ظمأة مني ، وقد

translationEn: The poet calls to Yusuf ibn Muhammad, a man whose giving hands and bright face never disappoint those who seek them out. He praises Yusuf's warlike reputation against the Byzantines, whose kings have fallen to his sword, and suggests the sea itself could barely contain his reach. The poet confesses that since parting from this patron, he has been in poverty and forced to praise liars — Yusuf's generosity is the only true sustenance.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Ceyhan river of Anatolia (frontier region)
drawing up, pulling from a well
liars, deceivers (whom one is forced to praise)

context: A praise poem for Yusuf ibn Muhammad, a frontier commander, contrasting the glorious past with the poet's current hardship. The river Seyhan places the addressee on the Byzantine frontier, linking personal patronage to the defense of Islam.

titleEn: We Offer Ourselves for You: Time Is Relentless

verses.ajuz: فمن منزل رحب ومن منزل ضنك

verses.sadr: وما أنت بالمهزوز جأشا على الأذى ،
على أنه قد ضيم في حبسك الهدى ،
verses.ajuz: وأضحى بك الإسلام في قبضة الشرك

verses.sadr: أما في نبي الله يوسف أسوة
أقام جميل الصبر في السجن برهة ،
verses.ajuz: فآل به الصبر الجميل إلى الملك

translationEn: The poet offers himself as a ransom for a patron who suffers in prison. He consoles him: time alternates between spacious and narrow stations, and adversity refines a man as smelting purifies pure gold. He draws a famous parallel — the Prophet Joseph, imprisoned unjustly, endured with beautiful patience, and that patience ultimately led him to kingship. The same destiny awaits this nobly suffering prisoner.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may we be your ransom, we sacrifice ourselves for you
one shaken in courage, easily frightened
a period of time, a while

context: A poem of consolation for a patron unjustly imprisoned, using the Quranic story of the Prophet Joseph as the central consoling image. This combination of classical allusion with personal sympathy shows al-Buhturi's range beyond pure panegyric.

titleAr: لو يكون الحباء حسب الذي أنت
titleEn: Were Gifts Commensurate to Your Worth

verses.sadr: لو يكون الحباء حسب الذي أنت

translationEn: The poet says that if gifts were proportional to what the addressee truly deserves, he would be showered with silver, pearls, and rubies scattered by the handful — and still that would fall short. He closes with a gracious note: a noble, refined person accepts the apology of a friend of modest means.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a person of little means, the poor

context: A brief three-verse gem of apology and flattery, gracefully excusing a modest gift by exalting the recipient's worth beyond any material measure. Al-Buhturi's economy of expression here is remarkable.

titleEn: May My Father Be Your Ransom

verses.sadr: غير أني رددت برك ، إذ كان

translationEn: The poet ransoms his father for his patron, declaring him worthy of all piety and noble endeavor. He notes that a small gift grows large in the eyes of one who hopes for it, while a large gift can seem small. He playfully refuses a returned favor, saying it would constitute forbidden usury. He ends with a witty point: if poetry is repaid with poetry of equal worth, any money given is surplus.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may my father be your ransom (expression of devotion)
the one who hopes in you, your seeker
usury, interest on a loan (forbidden in Islam)
the gold coins are surplus, extra beyond what is owed

context: A charming four-verse piece playing on the economics of poetic exchange and gift-giving. The humor about usury and the equation of equal verse with fair payment gives this short poem a pleasingly intellectual wit.

titleEn: Tell the Cloud Driven by the North Wind

verses.sadr: لا تكذبن ، فأنت ألطف في الحشا
لو شئت عدت إلى التناصب في الهوى ،
إن الرعية لم تزل في سيرة
هي أفضل الرتب ، التي جعلت له

verses.ajuz: في ظل ملك ادركوا ما أملوا

verses.sadr: قد نافس الغيب الحضور على الذي

translationEn: The poem opens with the classical cloud-messenger motif: the poet tells the north-wind-driven cloud to stop at Aleppo and greet his beloved Alawwa. He confesses his contradictory love — he approaches while she retreats, he gives love freely while she withholds. The ode pivots to praise of the Caliph al-Mutawakkil as the inheritor of Umaric governance, a forgiving king who awed even the Byzantine envoys into speechless reverence at his banquet. Their hands trembled with astonishment as they tried to eat, overwhelmed by the Caliph's glory.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the banquet table, the spread of food
confused, bewildered (people of Dhuhl tribe; here: astonished)

context: One of al-Buhturi's major odes praising al-Mutawakkil, famous for its vivid account of the Byzantine delegation's astonishment at the Caliph's court. The cloud-messenger device (risala) opening is a literary sophistication that elevates the poem beyond standard panegyric.

titleEn: Were You Not to Rebuke Me, I Would Say

verses.ajuz: حرقا توقد في الحشا ، ما ترحل
نشوان يجمل فيه ما لا يجمل

verses.sadr: يا دار ! لا زالت رباك مجودة ،
وسألت من لا يستجيب فكنت في

verses.ajuz: من قبل أن يقع القضاء ، فتعقل

verses.sadr: لم تنصرف عنها النفوس ولم تزغ
أوما ترى حسن الزمان ، وما بدا ،
من بعد ما اسود النهار المنتضى

verses.ajuz: من دهرنا ، ما لم يكن يتسهل

verses.sadr: إن كل صرف الدهر لم يكلل ، وإن

translationEn: The poem opens with a reluctant pause at the ruins of Rama — the poet says that but for the blamer's reproach he would say more of his longing. Tears precede him but burning grief stays behind. He then celebrates the accession of al-Mutawakkil: the caliphate came to Ja'far willingly without a spear or sword, led by his merit and drawn by his approaching fortune. Under his rule darkness brightened, the parched earth grew moist, and the age of injustice gave way to verdant justice. God and the Prophet's Messenger himself thank the Caliph's sincere stewardship.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rights and dues that had set, gone into darkness
they rose like stars, appeared again

context: A major panegyric celebrating al-Mutawakkil's accession to the caliphate, framed with a personal nasib at Rama's ruins. The theological dimensions — linking the Caliph to the Prophet's sunna and the Quran's approval — are among the most elevated in al-Buhturi's panegyric corpus.

titleEn: People Warn Each Other Away from Me

verses.ajuz: أبي ، وابن همام بن مرة خالي

translationEn: People prudently warn each other away from the poet, but the Tahirid rival still insults him, as if unaware of the poet's noble lineage — his father Buhtur and his uncle Ibn Hammam ibn Murra. The poet asserts his family's virtue matches or exceeds the rival's. He contrasts their settings: the rival has olive groves, the poet has tamarisk and willow. He ends with a pointed request: give him his poetry's worth, for his poetry is all his wealth.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: they warn each other off, they deter one another
he vies with me, competes with me
an equal to their nobles, a peer
give me, hand over to me

context: A poem of tribal boasting and gentle complaint against a Tahirid rival, ending with a frank request for fair compensation for the poet's work. The comparison of olive groves to tamarisk is a gentle assertion of equal worth between different tribal traditions.

titleAr: أبى الليل ، إلا أن يعود بطوله
titleEn: The Night Insists on Returning with Its Length

verses.sadr: أبى الليل ، إلا أن يعود بطوله

verses.ajuz: فيقلع ، أو يشفى جوى من غليله
بنا البعد من حزن الفلا وسهوله

verses.sadr: إلى أن بدا صحن العراق ، وكشفت

verses.ajuz: غدا العيش غضا بعد طول ذبوله

verses.sadr: أتى من بلاد الغرب في عدد النقا ،

verses.ajuz: وفي كل نفس حاجة من قفوله

verses.sadr: دعاه الهوى في سر من راء فانكفا
على أنها قد كان بدل طيبها ،
غدا ، وهو فرد في الفضائل كلها ،
verses.ajuz: فهل مخبر عن مثله ، أو عديله

verses.sadr: وإن ولاة العهد في الحلم والتقى ،

translationEn: The night refuses to end for the love-sleepless poet, who travels across the desert until Iraq's plain opens before him with its water and palm groves, as the doves recall his beloved with their cooing. He then celebrates the Caliph al-Mutawakkil's return to Samarra from the west, noting that Baghdad dressed in its finest for his arrival while the Caliph's own longing pulled him back to the Ja'fari palace. The poem ends with congratulations to the Caliph's son Muhammad on his father's return — a man unequaled in all virtues.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the curtains of darkness, veils of night
the Ja'fari palace (al-Mutawakkil's palace in Samarra)
his thicket, his lair (of a lion)

context: A qasida celebrating the Caliph al-Mutawakkil's return to Samarra. The poem opens with a personal journey across the desert, the landscape of Iraq serving as transition from private longing to public celebration. The description of Baghdad adorning itself for the Caliph is one of al-Buhturi's most memorable urban descriptions.

titleEn: After Mubashshir and Abu Ubayd Are Gone
translationEn: After the deaths of Mubashshir, Abu Ubayd, and the others of distinguished nobility, the poet asks how he can still trust in fate's loyalty or in the promises of nights. The elders of Banu Ubayd have abandoned him to a quarter emptied of his peers. He inherited their swords, but they have gone nobly — and swords without the men who wield them are useless.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: time's faithfulness, fate keeping its promises
peers, equals in rank and quality

context: A brief elegy on departed companions from Banu Ubayd, combining grief for lost friends with a bitter observation on the uselessness of inherited weapons without the men to bear them. Al-Buhturi's elegies, though fewer than his panegyrics, show remarkable concision.

titleEn: I Saw al-Bahbahani Burdened by Deprivation
translationEn: The poet observes al-Bahbahani setting out burdened by great deprivation; whenever this man tries to cultivate finer manners his old nature pulls him back. He wept seeing his hopes as faded ruins. The poet warns him: your quarreling with people is unwise, your singing is off-tune, and your social errors double. The poet's advice is measured: the recklessness of a fool is less dangerous to a noble man than the quiet resentment of a wise one finally pushed too far.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: his riding beasts set out, he departed
you behave rudely, you brawl while drunk
strikes fire, ignites (metaphor for kindling resentment)

context: A poem of advice and mild satire directed at al-Bahbahani, combining observation of his failures with wise counsel about the greater danger of provoking the patience of a generous man. The final verse is one of al-Buhturi's most quotable aphorisms.

titleEn: Do the Ruins of Salma at Kadhima Still Stand

verses.ajuz: إن لم يجد جرما لدي تجرما
بالصب في سنة الكرى ، ما سلما

verses.sadr: تأبى رباه أن تجيب ، ولم يكن
ولو أن نجدة ذاك ، أو هذا
قد كان آن لمغمد أن ينتضى
لم يغب عن شيء فيطلبه ، ولم

verses.ajuz: إذ كنت لا ألقاك إلا منعما

verses.sadr: قد طال بي عهد وهز جوانحي

translationEn: The poem opens with the poet addressing the ruins of Salma at Kadhima, asking if they can quench the thirst of a wandering lover or support one oppressed by longing. He weeps at traces now as illegible as foreign script. He then praises Banu al-Mudabbir — two brothers like twin rains, one settled in Iraq and one in the west, both as generous as the star Shi'ra. He singles out Ahmad ibn Muhammad, a man whose courage advances where cowards retreat, who is a guiding light in confusion and who crushed the greatest tyrant of the land. The poem closes with warm greetings to Abu Ishaq, whose open cheerfulness dispels the poet's gloom like sunlight breaking through clouds, and whose friendship has been as constant as spring since ancient times.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Kadhima, a coastal location in eastern Arabia associated with longing
brownish tinge of the lips (a mark of beauty)
the star Sirius (associated with abundant rain)
they submitted utterly, they acknowledged fully

context: A major multi-section qasida that moves from a love-ruins nasib at Kadhima through praise of the Banu al-Mudabbir brothers and the warrior Ahmad ibn Muhammad, before concluding with affectionate greeting to Abu Ishaq. The poem's range of tone — from elegiac to martial to warmly personal — exemplifies al-Buhturi's technical versatility.

titleEn: Tell the South Wind: Carry My Message

verses.sadr: كرم الزمان ولمت فيك ولن ترى
قد زاد يوم البؤس بعدك ، إنه
لا كان وجدي أين كان ، وانت لي

translationEn: Tell the south wind: when you set out at dawn, carry to my burning heart a breath of fragrance from the abode of Nasim. You deceived me — yet you are a moon that deceives the night of its darkness and clouds. Time has been generous, and I have blamed you for it; you will see no wonder greater than time's generosity or my reproach. I wronged my own soul by striving against itself — so hear the regret of one who is both wrongdoer and wronged. The day of misery has grown worse since you left, for it has come to me on the heels of a day of bliss. You have settled in my heart while your form travels on — may neither the traveler nor the one who stays be far from you. Let my longing be as it is, wherever it may be, for you are my sovereign and my bond with you is no dishonor. Do I now dare hope for union, when between us stands the watcher's eye and the gate of Ibrahim?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my liver; figuratively, the seat of intense feeling

context: A tender ghazal in which al-Buhturi asks the south wind to carry his longing to his beloved Nasim. The final verse strikes a note of bitter irony: he dares to dream of reunion even as a jealous watcher and a locked gate stand between them.

titleEn: Banu Mukhlid, Restrain Your Generosity
translationEn: O Banu Mukhlid, hold back the flood of your generosity, and do not cheat us of our share in noble deeds. And do not champion the glory of Qunan and Malik by taking from us the reputation of Hatim.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Hatim al-Ta'i, the proverbial paragon of generosity

context: A pithy two-verse epigram in which al-Buhturi playfully addresses the Banu Mukhlid clan, teasing that their excessive generosity threatens to eclipse his own tribe's legendary reputation — invoking the name of Hatim al-Ta'i, the gold standard of Arab liberality.

titleAr: إذا شئت فاندبني إلى الراح ، وانعني
titleEn: Call Me to the Wine Cup When You Will

verses.sadr: إذا شئت فاندبني إلى الراح ، وانعني
verses.ajuz: إلى الشرب من ذي خلة ونديم
إلى كمد ، في الصدر ، غير سقيم

verses.sadr: فإن مت كان الموت من كرم الهوى ،
verses.ajuz: وليس الهوى ، إن لم أمت بكريم

verses.sadr: أبا الفضل ! راجع من حجاك ، فإنني

translationEn: When you wish, call me to the wine and mourn me to the company of a friend and drinking companion. Tip the clear glass away from me, for though I have stayed behind, my spirit is no longer present among you. My body is gripped by an illness that, whenever I seem to overcome it, draws me back into a grief lodged in the chest, untouched by disease. If I die, my death will be a sign of love's nobility — for love that does not kill is no true love. Tell the rose-scented breeze: be gone, for I spurn you out of reverence for the face of Nasim. I repented, and people asked how I could abandon him — say this blame falls upon one who deserves blame. O Abu al-Fadl, return to the one who kept you back, for I am in great peril from what I most fear. You told me that consolation is a mark of dignity — but does any man seek consolation from such a one unless he is base? The distance between us is not great, nor is the covenant between us old.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a breeze; also a proper name (his beloved)

context: A wine and love poem addressed to Abu al-Fadl, interweaving the poet's bodily illness, his obsessive love for Nasim, and an invitation to the wine circle. The paradox that dying of love is noble while surviving loveless is ignoble is a hallmark of Abbasid ghazal.

titleEn: Rain Blessed al-Dhafafi's Land
translationEn: May the heaped clouds pour their rain upon al-Dhafafi's land in the darkness of its nights. He is a brother to me, though I share no kinship with his father or mother. He has changed so much that I can barely recognize him — I know nothing of him now but his name. What I retain of him is only a tenderness that refreshes poetry through its understanding. So too with musk: those who keep it have no use for it except its fragrance.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one is refreshed by it, it brings relief

context: A brief, quietly melancholic poem in which al-Buhturi mourns the estrangement of a friend — not through blood but through the bond of poetry. The closing musk simile is characteristic of al-Buhturi's lapidary style: the only value left in the friendship is its lingering, intangible fragrance.

titleEn: We Have Lost Loyalty Like a Dear Friend

verses.ajuz: أمس ، يا أحمد بن عبد الرحيم

translationEn: We have lost loyalty as one loses a dear friend, and we have mourned high ideals as one mourns ruined dwelling-places. I do not tire of blaming Time — it is enough to occupy me that I have condemned every blameworthy thing. Do you think that wealth is a worthy reward for a man of ambition who stands waiting at a miser's door? I find in the embarrassment of being turned away a great and terrible risk in the very act of asking. Sometimes the face of the miser is fairer to behold than the back of one who has been denied. But a generous man crossed my path and I stretched out my hand, seeking favor from a noble soul. He has won my praise — and the winds that bring the rain earn the same gratitude as the clouds themselves. This return after a first gift of yours that came just yesterday, O Ahmad ibn Abd al-Rahim — your patience with one who seemed suspect never made your face harsh before my need.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a dear friend; also boiling water; summer heat
seeking a gift, petitioning for generosity
one suspected, one thought poorly of
the deprived one, he who is turned away empty-handed

context: A panegyric addressed to Ahmad ibn Abd al-Rahim that opens with a lament on the loss of loyalty and noble values before turning to praise the patron's renewed generosity. The verse comparing the miser's face to the rejected petitioner's turned back is one of al-Buhturi's most memorably sardonic observations.

titleEn: A Dream-Vision Visits Me in Sleep
translationEn: A dream-vision that visits me in sleep is free of my burning passion and longing. A maiden for whom I have endured blame all night — and the lover's torment is the full length of reproach. She cast a stolen glance at me, and the corner of her eye infected my body with sickness. She is feminine, then masculine — she has the coquetry of a tender girl and the strength of a young man. How much finer lawful beauty is when it resembles in hearts the charm of what is forbidden. She gave me to drink, from her cup and from her lips, what quenches the thirst of one consumed by longing. In a season of temperate weather that rivals her in balance, echoing her erect and perfect posture. True life is when the nights outstretch the days in length. The air was clear, and the cool sweetness of water delighted in blending with wine. The fair pool was brought to completion at the finest hour, a home of intimacy and a dwelling of permanence. It gazed upon the handsome face and, had it been able, would have greeted it openly with salutation. Both were clothed in splendor, each facing the other — one laughing, the other with a radiant smile, like two lovers who, had they been able to meet, would have embraced and clasped each other without end. The wind threads its course between the pool's two sides and stumbles from weariness and tedium. Fed by a stream from the surging flood of the sky, it gleams like a polished white sword. When it reaches the center of the beautiful basin, the water casts upon it the hue of marble. You see it as though it were sea water, deceiving the eye — yet it is only rainwater. And the water-wheels, when they turn, have no walking driver but the ostriches. These are wonders built for the foremost servants of God — by the corner, al-Safa, and the Maqam. The finest of palaces now glories in the hatred of its enemies toward the best of mankind. It stands near the Ja'fari palace, and Shabdaz drew close to it like one who desires and craves. The palace of the king's abodes gleams like stars shining in the blackness of night. They defy description, grasped only by conjecture and imagining — we sense them only in our hopes, or see them only in the dreams that come at night. These are lofty chambers of religion and this world built together — in which God ordains the reward of the Imam. They have stirred our longing for paradise and made us more devoted to avoiding sins and transgressions. Through them the early rulers drank of sovereignty and vied in glory with those who sought to outnumber Islam. May God bless the Caliph in his exalted glory and his great deeds and exploits. May He show him the fulfillment of his hopes in the crown-princes, the people of loyalty and beneficence. May they remain always in happiness and joy, in endurance and continuity from his reign.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a dream-vision, the apparition of the beloved in sleep
surging flood, vast expanse of water
things that silence description, overwhelming beauties

context: A long and architecturally ambitious qasida that moves from a ghazal prelude through a vivid wasf of a royal palace pool and water-wheels, culminating in a panegyric for the Caliph. The poem is notable for al-Buhturi's characteristic nature-description, here applied to built environment and hydraulic marvels.

titleAr: أعن جوار أبي إسحاق تطمع أن
titleEn: Do You Seek to Move Me from Abu Ishaq?

verses.sadr: أعن جوار أبي إسحاق تطمع أن
verses.ajuz: تزيل رحلي ، يا بهل بن بهلانا

verses.sadr: عهد من الأنس عاقرنا الكؤوس على

translationEn: Do you aspire, O Bahl ibn Bahlana, to uproot my lodging from the neighborhood of Abu Ishaq? You propose to me a losing bargain — had I ever accepted it, I would have consigned the tribes of Lakhm and Ghassan to guarantee it. You contrived from your far corner to dupe me, using Banu al-Mudabbir as your supporters and allies. People are content to accept them as lords of their nobility — so how should I make them angry, O Bahl, as brothers? Grant that I were rich enough in my own wealth to need nothing of their giving — what then would I do with the old companionship that was? A bond of intimacy over which we drained our cups from its very beginning, wandering through its seasons together. We are now too mature to abandon it in middle age — we who spent our youth traversing those days through it. True friends whom I never deceived in their affection, never called upon for anything hard or easy alike. I would never sell my freedom to become their servant out of greed, while you, O Bahl, seek them for nothing. Be off — you shall win no favor from what you know, nor shall you achieve the possibility of what you have attempted.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my camp, my lodging, my mount's saddle
a losing deal, a bad bargain
to dupe me by moonlight, to deceive me
for free, without payment or effort

context: A defiant poem in which al-Buhturi rejects an unnamed adversary's attempt to pry him away from the patronage of Abu Ishaq and the friendship of Banu al-Mudabbir. The poem is unusual for its sharp conversational register, defending old loyalty against mercenary opportunism.

titleEn: Take Up Longing or Bear Its Weight

verses.sadr: حمت قضب المجد من أن تكون

translationEn: Take up longing willingly or suffer it under compulsion, so that you may be excused in the severity of its griefs. My heartache has not moved a lock of hair that passes through the changes of its colors. The first strands of white which the blamer points to and magnifies in importance — when pleasure is forbidden for their sake, their worth rises beyond measure. If you do not find me fully obedient to them, it is not because I have disobeyed them in every way. Whenever a disaster comes in love, its secrets remain hidden before its public disclosure. Men turned blind eyes to noble deeds even when they stood displayed before them, and paid no heed to what duty required, though obligation rang in their very ears. I turned away my hand — arm bent away — from false affections and their betrayals. My friend already knew that I would part from her when she abandoned me. I find my heart more at ease in the abodes of the noble and their homelands. My soul kept its distance from her wealth yet never kept it from the wealth of brothers. I have settled upon Abu Ghalib as my companion for breaking calamities and wearing them down. Persia counts among his honors a nearness to Khusraw son of Sasan. When asked about him in moments of pride, she speaks with the most truthful knowledge. They boast of him through the pupil of their eye, and the eye has its own pride in its pupil. We tore through the veil of darkness to reach him, on lean camels racing their riders. We undertook the distance of Qumm and Kashan to seek the farewell. The apparition of the girl of Samira smiled, baring white and gleaming teeth. When it looked eastward to the glittering of the snows, it submitted to him before its proper season. So one who would be lost fulfills his helpers above the clouds and their reins. For the king's sake the necks of praise were locked away at their full price. You were shielded from fate by souls in twos and ones, enviers and individuals alike. Glory grows dim when your two hands are not there to kindle its fires. You are lavish among the people until your deeds become the truest of their helpers. You guarded the branches of glory from becoming kindling for the hardness of their wood. Through you, a soul that had run its long course toward praise turned away from blame. The gifts are made precious by their repetition and by the long display of giving them twice over. I see generosity when it is scarce as different from generosity when it is easy. The finest deed for the noble is their beneficence at the very time they receive beneficence. None aspires to noble deeds and reaches their summit except the true horsemen of virtue. For whoever returned from your courts after me bearing a shortfall in my share and its loss — my avoidance of you was itself one of the sins, so my turning toward you deserves its forgiveness. No group was ever punished if it trusted in its former faithlessness after belief. For the final seals of deeds are what you see: the crowning summations of their religions.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: white hair, the onset of old age
its knights, the true masters of noble deeds

context: A complex long qasida opening with an amatory prelude (nasib) reflecting on longing, white hair, and false friendships before pivoting to panegyric of Abu Ghalib, a Persian-descended patron associated with the Sasanian heritage. The journey to Qumm and Kashan grounds the poem in the eastern Iranian world of Abbasid patronage.

titleAr: ملنا ، أم نبا بنا ، أم جفانا ،
titleEn: Did We Tire, or Did He Abandon Us?

verses.sadr: ملنا ، أم نبا بنا ، أم جفانا ،

verses.ajuz: أل عن سخطنا ، ولا عن رضانا

verses.sadr: ما لنا نعبد العباد إذا كا

translationEn: Did we grow weary, or did he veer away from us, or did he forsake us — or did he come to despise us and replace us with others? He is one we seek to please while he is angry, yet he does not ask after our anger or our contentment. We care about not seeing one given to wrongdoing, yet Time cares nothing whether it sees us or not. Excuse is confined in supplication — for if we were content with our allotted share, it would be enough for us. Why do we worship servants when our poverty and our wealth both lie with God?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: veered away from us, repelled us
he substituted, he replaced (one thing for another)
we worship servants (i.e., prostrate ourselves before people)

context: The opening five verses of a longer qasida, preserved here due to OCR corruption in the source beyond verse 5. These verses form a complete thematic unit: a meditation on the humiliation of dependence on patrons, culminating in the resonant rhetorical question about why one worships human beings when all wealth and poverty belong to God alone.

titleAr: ليت الخليط الذي قد بان لم يبن
titleEn: Would That the Departed Companions Had Not Left

verses.sadr: ليت الخليط الذي قد بان لم يبن
verses.ajuz: وليت ما كان من حبيك لم يكن
عين ، بكت شجوها من منظر حسن

verses.sadr: يا نظرة لي من الشمس التي طلعت
ما أحسن الصبر ، إلا عند فرقة من
ما تقع العين منها حين تلحظها ،
verses.ajuz: إلا على فتنة من أقتل الفتن
حتى كأن قضيب البان لم يلن

verses.sadr: لي عن قليل لا يلم به
إن الهموم ، إذا أوطن في خلد
verses.ajuz: للمرء ، سار ولم يربع على وطن
آذي دجلة في عير من السفن
هاد من الماء ، منقاد بلا رسن
يدي أبي الفضل أو في نائل الحسن

verses.sadr: نهدي القريض إلى رب القريض معا

verses.ajuz: أبقى على الزمن الباقي من الزمن

verses.sadr: شكر امرىء ظل مشغولا بشكرك عن
قد قلت إذ بسطت كفاك من أملي :
verses.ajuz: ما شاء من نائبات الدهر ، فليكن

verses.sadr: وزدتني رغبة في عقد ودك ، إذ
من يصبه سكن ممن يحب ، ومن
verses.ajuz: يهوى ، فما لك غير الجود من سكن

verses.sadr: بدني إلى الجود كفا منك قد أنست

translationEn: Would that the companions who have departed had not departed, and would that what was of love between us had never been. Most deserving of weeping is an eye that cried its grief over a beautiful sight now gone. What a glance I had from that sun which rose among those departing, amid the flock of soft-haired women. How fine is patience — except at the parting from one whose grief has made me dwell between grief and sorrow. A sand dune upon whose summit stands a branch, and a sun hidden in clouds at the top of that branch. Wherever the eye falls upon her when it glances, it lands only on a temptation that is the most deadly of temptations. She stood up swaying, growing soft in her frame, until it seemed the willow bough itself had ceased to bend. Soon I shall have a longing for you that is not too slight to be blamed, and a heart that is not yet fully pledged. When sorrows settle in a man's heart as a permanent abode, he travels on and does not pause at any homeland. We brought al-Muhadhdhab Ibrahim through the current of the Tigris on a caravan of vessels. The winds of distant quarters drove them on, and a guide of water led them without a bridle. We came to you bearing ornate words, as though their embroidery were woven from the right-hand cloth of Yemen. As though they walked the proud swaggering gait in the hands of Abu al-Fadl or in the gifts of grace. We offer poetry to the lord of poetry — like one bearing fine cloth as a gift to Aden itself. Every bright poem, like a radiant flower, more lasting on enduring time than time itself. The gratitude of a man who has remained so occupied with thanking you that he neglects even excessive weeping over ruins and abandoned campgrounds. I said when you stretched out both hands from my hopes: let whatever the reversals of fate may will, come as they may. I was content with your character, which has mingled with noble deeds as the soul mingles with the body. You increased my desire for the bond of your friendship when you paired that generosity with understanding and keen intelligence. Whoever finds peace from those he loves — and whoever loves — has no shelter but your liberality. My body has grown accustomed to your generous hand, which has grown familiar with giving and grace, as the eye grows familiar with sleep.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the companions who share a camp; those who have departed together
the current of the Tigris, its waves

context: A polished qasida addressed to al-Muhadhdhab Ibrahim, combining a ghazal prelude describing the beloved's beauty with a vivid account of travelling the Tigris by boat to reach the patron. The poem closes with compliments that pair generosity with intellect — a refinement typical of al-Buhturi's high-Abbasid panegyric.

titleEn: Sleeplessness for My Eye's Delight
translationEn: My eye is kept sleepless because the nights have come between the joy of my eye and me. If time decrees that we shall meet, that decree will be my judgment over time itself and my creed. Nothing has the brightness of one thing compared with another as a swift reunion after separation.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the coolness of my eye; the delight of my sight, my beloved
my religion, my creed; what I owe

context: A compact three-verse gem of ghazal poetry, expressing the sleeplessness caused by separation from the beloved and the ecstatic brightness that reunion would bring. The middle verse — that reunion would be al-Buhturi's judgment over time itself — has the characteristic epigrammatic compression of his shorter lyric pieces.

titleEn: Tell Abu Jafar: His Hand Reaches Far

verses.sadr: كم لك فيها من نائل ويد ،

translationEn: Tell Abu Jafar — for he has a hand whose reach extends to the most distant recipient of its giving. The hand of rain refuses to rival it, and time falls short in trying to match its length. You have remained among the Qasit tribe, and so the hillocks of glory and high honor remain for it. You held back by your sharp blades those who would wrong it, and raised up by your noble deeds those who depend on it. Your fortified deeds are counted — if a tribe were to count its strongholds. How many gifts and hands have you given to it, by which you have dominated both Bakr and Wa'il. Am I to broadcast your generosity? It would be like one who conceals a downpour and its heavy rain. Behold — this is a favor which, when it is mentioned, becomes a gift and you become its bestower. None can undertake to complete its latter half except a noble man who began its first. You were swift in beginning your beneficence — now be its one who comes late in its renewal.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its giving, its reach, its gift
to rival it, to draw water from the same bucket in competition
one who wrongs it, its wrongdoer
those who depend on it, its dependents
a sudden heavy shower, a downpour
its one who hastened it, who was prompt in it

context: A tightly-structured panegyric addressed to Abu Jafar, likely a chief of the Qasit tribe of northern Arabia. The poem praises his protective role within his tribe through military and financial generosity, and closes with a delicate request for a renewed gift, framed as the completion of a virtue already begun.

titleEn: The Ignoble One Satirized Me

verses.ajuz: وفاء ، إذا كان لا أصل له
ت وما لم يكن لك أن تفعله

translationEn: The ignoble wretch has satirized me — and I never imagined I should fear the satire of Abu Harmala. I had been at length in praising him and establishing his tangled genealogy. I hoped his changeability would turn to candor, and I met his estrangement with reconciliation. I took him to be loyal — but how could he have loyalty when he has no noble root? Do not praise the last chapter of a companion if you have not tested his first. If he has proved contrary to my expectation of him and strayed from the covenant or exchanged it — I was not the first to lose at a companion's hands some part of what he had hoped. Did I not distinguish you with the affection and complete love you knew full well? And I asked on your behalf Abu Salih, when it was not your right that I should have to ask him. I was told that you deserved the kindness of high station and honored rank. And my reward was what you know full well — and what was not yours to do. I see you have returned to your noble grandfather and your insoluble case. And his nightly dwelling was in the belly of a wicker basket, a worn-out palm-leaf container. When it darkened behind its weave, one imagined it a clay pot in a fruit-basket. God witness his state in the morning, found discarded on the rubbish heap. Flies assembled their squadrons over him, covering him swarm upon swarm. There, if Qushayr were to claim him, none would imagine she was lying.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I was at length, I elaborated extensively
a small basket for fruit, a date container
squadrons, divisions (of an army; here used for fly-swarms)
a swarm, a dense mass (of insects)

context: A sharp hija' (satire) targeting a man of obscure birth who dared to compose a lampoon against al-Buhturi. After a prelude of disappointed friendship, the poem descends into vivid comic ridicule of the target's ancestor sleeping in a worn wicker basket and being found on a rubbish heap, a deliberately degrading image.

titleEn: A Young Man's Worth Is Not in Show
translationEn: O Abu Jafar, a young man's excellence does not lie in going out in excess of self-admiration, nor in the sleekness of his horse, nor in the cleanliness of his garments. Rather it lies in noble deeds and the most distinguished and outstanding esteem. I see that you desire to acquire praise yet do not know the measure of what makes it obligatory. How do you hope to arrive at it when you have not worked through its means? If I seek a reward through it, you are not one who can fulfil the request. And if I offer it as an act of piety, the poor are more deserving of it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the excellence of a young man, his virtue and worth
distinguished, eminent, one who stands out
an act done for God's reward, a pious offering
one who is capable, one who can fulfill an obligation

context: A pointed didactic poem addressed to Abu Jafar, gently but firmly instructing him that true nobility lies in action, not in display. The final verse — that if al-Buhturi were to give his poetry as charity, the poor would be more deserving than this patron — is a memorably biting conclusion that barely veils its insult.

titleEn: The Story of the Mound: Hear Its Wonders

verses.sadr: إحفروا التل يا بني عبد الأع

translationEn: The story of the mound — hear its wonders, for in tales like it speeches grow long. Two factions disputed the mound and fell into contention: the clan of Abd al-A'la and the clan of Thawaba. The just judge al-Junaydi ruled among them rightly — may we never lose his rightness. Dig up the mound, O Banu Abd al-A'la, and stir its rocks and its soil. If you find in it your father's fishing nets, you are its masters before all others. Or if you find cupping instruments when you dig, the doubt of the suspicious group will be resolved. Then appeared a dark wicker box containing the old man's tools — and he is the grandfather of Lababah.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the mound, a raised earthen hillock
they contended, they fell into dispute
cupping vessels, instruments of the cupper
a dark-colored wicker box or basket
palm fronds, palm leaves used for weaving
the old man's tools, his trade instruments

context: A humorous narrative poem about a legal dispute over a mound of earth, settled by a judge whose verdict amounts to: dig it up and see whose tools you find. The punch line reveals the ancestor's belongings were a cupper's instruments — a lowly trade — and the satirical intent is clear: al-Buhturi is mocking the pretensions of a family of barber-surgeons.

titleEn: God Bless You, O Jalalata
translationEn: God bless you, O Jalalata — and what a production you have brought forth among the people of the secretarial craft. You have transferred the condition of Banu Thawaba from lancets and razors to pens.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: God bless your origin; an exclamation of wonder or irony
the scribal arts, the secretarial profession
lancets, surgical blades (of a cupper or surgeon)
pens, reed pens (of a scribe or secretary)

context: A satirical two-verse epigram, a continuation of the mockery of Banu Thawaba from the preceding poem. Al-Buhturi sardonically congratulates 'Jalalata' — apparently a matriarch of the family — for raising her sons from the lowly trade of cupping and barbering to the respectable profession of secretarial scribes.

titleEn: Time Is Bad and People Are Worse
translationEn: Time is rotten, and all of creation is wind. If you ask them for generosity, their reply to that is merely noise. If they could hoard the light they would, so that no light would remain for the rest of creation. The generous ones have all gone, and left us only 'if only' and 'if'.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rotten, bad, wretched (colloquial/short form of سوء)
wind, emptiness, nothing (a straw-stuffed skin; an empty thing)
an expressive sound of dismissal or refusal; noise, nothing
they hoard, they keep close control over
would that; if only — expressions of futile regret

context: A witty and cynical four-verse epigram in a rhyme scheme that uses single-syllable words (سو، بو، وو، ضو، لو) to comic effect, lamenting the disappearance of generous men and the worthlessness of those who remain. The poem is notable for its deliberately simple, almost colloquial vocabulary deployed with high ironic intent.

titleAr: لنا أبدا يث نعانيه في أروى ،
titleEn: We Endure Eternal Grief for Arwa

verses.sadr: لنا أبدا يث نعانيه في أروى ،
verses.ajuz: وحزوى ، وكم أدنتك من لوعة حزوى

verses.sadr: وما كان دمعي قبل أروى بنهزة
verses.ajuz: لأدنى خليط بان أو منزل أقوى
أمارة برح الحب أن تكثر الشكوى

translationEn: We endure eternally a grief we suffer for Arwa and Hazwa — and how many times has Hazwa brought you close to heartache's burning. My tears before Arwa were never easily provoked by any parting companion or abandoned campsite. When the heart finds relief in kindness — whichever of the two is brighter — a sick heart has attached itself to her, and through her it is healed. I have multiplied my complaints of love for her — for the true sign of the severity of love is the multiplication of complaint.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Hazwa, a place name associated with longing in Arabic poetry
burning heartache, the searing pain of love
an easy prey, a ready opportunity; here: easily provoked
the severity of love, love's torment

context: A brief and elegant ghazal on love for Arwa, notable for its closing maxim — that the very excess of a lover's complaint is proof of the depth of his love. The poem plays on the double meaning of healing (يدوى): the sick heart is both wounded by and cured through its attachment to the beloved.

titleEn: The First Rains Came at Dawn

verses.sadr: يا إمام الهدى ، ويا صاحب الحق ،

translationEn: The first rains of the early season came to us at dawn, then departed, and in their wake came the spring rains. I see the rain ceaselessly pouring on a dewy morning and an evening. The rain has given the earth its full drink from its abundance — so give me my full drink from the unmixed wine. Joy of bliss appeared at morning and remained at evening between the palace of al-Sabih and the Ja'fari palace. In the wonderful edifice, the intimate dwelling, the beautiful and radiant prospect. And gardens that souls long for, and that are given life by the freshness of their blooms. A royal abode chosen for an Imam whose hand has secured the inheritance of the Prophet. God has given the community from him the conduct of the virtuous, the pious, the intelligent. The community is endowed with his generous beneficence spread upon it and the wisdom of the one who is approved. O Imam of guidance, O possessor of rightful authority, O son of al-Rashid and al-Mahdi — may your era, beloved among the people, endure with a life that lasts and a livelihood of contentment.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the first rain of the season, the autumn-onset rain
the second seasonal rain, the spring rain following the first
the inheritance of the Prophet, the prophetic legacy
the approved one, the one with whom God is pleased
the Ja'fari palace, an Abbasid palace in Baghdad

context: A panegyric for an Abbasid caliph — son of al-Rashid and grandson of al-Mahdi — framed by a wasf of seasonal rains and the splendors of the royal palaces between al-Sabih and the Ja'fari. The poem is a characteristic al-Buhturi composition: natural description of rain at dawn transitions seamlessly into architectural description of the palace gardens and thence into royal panegyric.
